title: [Mined from SocratiCode] Two-tier progress tracking with resumable batch checkpo
text:
[Mined from SocratiCode] Two-tier progress tracking with resumable batch checkpoints: The indexer tracks in-memory progress for live status reporting, while also checkpointing file hashes to Qdrant after every batch. This makes long-running operations resumable after crashes or disconnects without redoing completed work. The same pattern appears in the graph builder with separate progress and completion records, giving a consistent operational model across subsystems.

CLAW has checkpointing concepts, but this repo shows a practical batch-boundary design with explicit resume semantics and persisted status that survives restarts.

## Two-tier progress tracking with resumable batch checkpoints

**Category:** architecture
**Source:** SocratiCode
**Relevance:** 0.95

### Description
The indexer tracks in-memory progress for live status reporting, while also checkpointing file hashes to Qdrant after every batch. This makes long-running operations resumable after crashes or disconnects without redoing completed work. The same pattern appears in the graph builder with separate progress and completion records, giving a consistent operational model across subsystems.

### Implementation Sketch
CLAW could use the same dual-layer progress model for repository enhancement jobs: an in-memory `JobProgress` map plus durable checkpoints in SQLite. Apply this to long-running tasks like analysis passes, patch application, and fleet scans in `src/services/*` and `src/state/*`, so interruptions resume from the last confirmed batch. Expose progress fields like `phase`, `filesProcessed`, `batchesProcessed`, and `durationMs` through the CLI and MCP responses.

### Augmentation Notes
CLAW has checkpointing concepts, but this repo shows a practical batch-boundary design with explicit resume semantics and persisted status that survives restarts.

inputs.name: job_progress_map
inputs.description: In-memory progress state for live status reporting, including per-job phase and counts.

inputs.name: batch_checkpoint_records
inputs.description: Durable checkpoint entries written after each batch, used to resume work after interruption.

inputs.name: file_hashes
inputs.description: Hashes of processed files or items used to identify completed work and avoid reprocessing.

inputs.name: execution_state
inputs.description: Current task execution context for long-running operations such as analysis passes, patch application, or scans.

outputs.name: live_progress_status
outputs.description: Up-to-date progress information exposed during execution, including fields like phase, filesProcessed, batchesProcessed, and durationMs.

outputs.name: durable_checkpoint_store_updates
outputs.description: Persisted checkpoint state saved to durable storage after each batch to support resumption.

outputs.name: resume_point
outputs.description: The last confirmed batch boundary from which interrupted jobs can safely continue without repeating completed work.

outputs.name: cli_mcp_progress_response
outputs.description: Progress and status payload returned through CLI and MCP interfaces.

source_artifacts.note: Batched checkpointing, cancellation boundaries, and persisted completion state
Incremental update with the same resumable checkpointing model
Persists file hashes and status after each batch

applicability: The indexer tracks in-memory progress for live status reporting, while also checkpointing file hashes to Qdrant after every batch. This makes long-running operations resumable after crashes or disconnects without redoing completed work. The same pattern appears in the graph builder with separate progress and completion records, giving a consistent operational model across subsystems.

CLAW could use the same dual-layer progress model for repository enhancement jobs: an in-memory `JobProgress` map plus durable checkpoints in SQLite. Apply this to long-running tasks like analysis passes, patch application, and fleet scans in `src/services/*` and `src/state/*`, so interruptions resume from the last confirmed batch. Expose progress fields like `phase`, `filesProcessed`, `batchesProcessed`, and `durationMs` through the CLI and MCP responses.
Long-running repository operations that may be interrupted by crashes or disconnects.
Batch-oriented workflows where completed work should not be repeated on restart.
Systems needing both live status updates and durable resume semantics.
Operational pipelines spanning multiple subsystems that benefit from a consistent progress model.
non_applicability: Requires adaptation to the target repo; do not apply blindly.
Short, atomic operations that complete before progress tracking or checkpointing provides value.
Workflows where partial completion cannot be safely resumed from batch boundaries.
Cases where durable storage is unavailable or checkpoint persistence would be unreliable.
Tasks that require exact real-time transactional consistency across every item rather than batch-level confirmation.
dependencies: SQLite/Qdrant test environment if integrating persistence
Existing job progress model in CLAW
durable storage backend such as SQLite
risks: CLAW has checkpointing concepts, but this repo shows a practical batch-boundary design with explicit resume semantics and persisted status that survives restarts.
Checkpoint/state divergence if in-memory progress and durable checkpoints are updated inconsistently.
Resuming from an incorrect batch boundary can duplicate work or skip items.
Persisting too infrequently can lose too much progress after failure.
Persisting too often can degrade throughput and increase storage overhead.
Exposed progress fields may mislead users if duration or counts are not updated atomically.
